title: Lesson 243
transcript: We've talked about DRP plans, disaster recovery plans, in a fair bit of detail. These are the plans that we use to recover specific systems or technologies. Let's now talk about how we test these plans. Because if you truly want to know if a deer play plan is actually going to work, you have to test it. And there's a bunch of different tests that we can do. So let's walk through each one of them and understand what is it that we do in these different types of tests. The first test is known as a read-through or checklist. Here's what that is. This is the person, the poor soul that's been chained to their desk in the basement for the last year writing this plan. This is where they simply read through the document, go through a checklist, of all the major components that should be contained within that DRP plan. Do we have all the right people named? Do we have phone numbers for them? Do we have alternate contact methods? All of these sort of components, the checklist, the read-through is where you make sure all those components are actually in the plan. So it's an entirely paper-based exercise, typically just done by the person that was creating the plan. Once you have successfully completed a read-through or checklist, then you can move on to a walk-through. A walkthrough is where you put all the various stakeholders around a table. Who are the various stakeholders? Are they relevant stakeholders? Well, this would be like the system owner, maybe someone from HR, from legal, maybe someone from senior executive, from media relations. you put all the relevant stakeholders around the table and you give them each a copy of the plan. And then you simply walk through the document. And because you've now got all the relevant stakeholders that are experts in their various areas, reading through this document, walking through this document, This is a good way of making sure that no pieces are missing, that it actually makes sense. So again, a walkthrough, just an entirely paper-based exercise. Once you've successfully done a walkthrough, then you can move on to a simulation. A simulation, again, looks very similar to a walkthrough. You typically put a bunch of people around a table, the various stakeholders, But the key differences in a simulation is you actually create some sort of scenario. And there's usually a couple people at the table that are running this scenario. And they'll be saying things, I've done this, it's quite fun. So you'll say things like, okay, now CNN is calling, or okay, now the worm is spreading across the network. What do you do now? And all the various stakeholders have to be reading through the plan, be figuring out what they're supposed to be doing, who should they be calling, what should they be doing. But very importantly, a simulation is still just an entirely paper-based exercise. You basically just put a sign on the door to say that, you know, we're going through a simulation. You do not touch any systems whatsoever. entirely paper-based. The next is a parallel test. After you've successfully completed a simulation, you can move on to a parallel test. Parallel test is very similar to a simulation. You're coming, you're simulating some sort of disaster, but importantly, in a parallel test, you actually touch some systems. you touch your backup or your redundant systems, you do not touch production systems. So in a parallel test, you're going through some sort of scenario and you're actually touching, you're actually recovering data, you're making backups on a parallel system. The final type of test is known as a full interruption, full scale test. And as the name implies, in a full scale, full interruption test, you're actually affecting not only parallel systems, but production systems. So in a full scale, full interruption test, you're actually causing a disaster and you're checking to see if your DRP plan truly works. Now, some people will sometimes say, well, do you actually need to do this? Or isn't this very dangerous? Yes, it is very dangerous. You can absolutely cause outages. And yes, you have to do this because if you truly wanna know if your systems are gonna work, it's critical to do this. Let me share a quick story. This is a true story. There was a bank, I won't even name where they are, but there was a bank and they wanted to test if their backup power system was actually going to work. Now, a quick bit of explanation here. This is a bank that had an office tower in a downtown setting. And typically when you have a data center, an office in a downtown setting, the generators will be up on the roof to make them accessible. And the fuel tanks will be in the basement. And there'll be big pumps that will pump fuel from the tanks up to the generators. Now this bank had done all the other tests, including a whole bunch of parallel testing. They'd run the generators for hours. They'd check the switching equipment. They were as sure as they could be that this backup system was going to work perfectly. So they decided to do a full interruption test. So on a Sunday night, middle of the night, when there was the least amount of activity, they decided to test their system. They turned off the power. and right away the ups provided instantaneous but short-term power the generators started to spin up they started to produce power the switching equipment all worked perfectly everything kept running And a few minutes in, everyone was starting to pat themselves on the back, like, woo, go team. You know, that was pretty scary, but we seem to have made it. And then, all the lights went out. The building went black. What had happened? Well, turns out, as I talked about, the fuel tanks are usually in the basement. The generators are on the roof. The generators typically have what are called day tanks. Day tanks are small fuel tanks directly connected to the generator and as these day tanks run out of fuel it sends a signal to the giant pumps in the basement to start moving more fuel up to the generators. The signal worked perfectly. The problem was the pumps were not connected to the backup power system. They were only connected to the mains. And the only way that they ever found this out was by doing a full DR test. So this is why it's so critical to do these types of tests. And that raises an important question. When and only when should you do a full interruption test? Well, you should only do it when all of the other tests have been completed successfully. And very important, CY, this is a bit of CYA, you have management's approval. Because it is absolutely possible that you can cause damage to systems, you can take things down. So you need to have management's approval to do this.
